1: All the congregation of the people of Israel moved on from the wilderness of Sin by stages, as YHWH commanded, and camped at Rephidim: Rephidim (r'phidim = supports/rest-places?) is the penultimate station before Sinai in the wilderness itinerary. The exact location is debated but is generally placed in the western Sinai near the approach to Jebel Musa or Jebel Serbal — the mountain candidates for Sinai/Horeb. The wilderness itinerary of the Exodus is the earliest geographical text in the Hebrew Bible and has been the subject of archaeological and geographical study for over a century, though no consensus on exact routes has been reached.
2: Therefore the people quarreled (va'yarev) with Moses and said, 'Give us water to drink.' And Moses said to them, 'Why do you quarrel with me? Why do you test (t'nasun) YHWH?' Rav (to quarrel/dispute) gives its name to the place Meribah (quarrel); nassah (to test) gives its name to the place Massah (testing). The two place-names (Exodus 17:7) encode the theological verdict on the water-complaint — the place is named for the community's failure, not their experience. The distinction between crying to YHWH in genuine need and testing YHWH's provision is a fine but significant one; the wilderness complaint-cycle navigates this distinction throughout.
3: But the people thirsted there for water, and the people grumbled against Moses and said, 'Why did you bring us up out of Egypt, to kill us and our children and our livestock with thirst?' The rhetoric of the complaint moves from the present crisis ('give us water') to a comprehensive accusation ('you brought us out to kill us'). The escalation from specific request to global accusation is the rhetorical pattern of panic-driven complaints throughout the Psalms' complaint genre (e.g., Ps 22). The inclusion of 'our children and our livestock' widens the complaint to cover the entire community's survival.
4: So Moses cried to YHWH, 'What shall I do with this people? They are almost ready to stone me (s'qol yisgal'uni).' Moses's intercessory prayer here is a complaint to YHWH about the people rather than a petition on their behalf — a 'complaint within a complaint' structure. The threat of stoning (s'qal = to stone/pelt) was a legitimate form of community justice in ancient Israel (cf. Numbers 14:10 — the congregation threatens to stone Moses and Aaron at Kadesh-Barnea in a near-identical situation). The community's frustration is at a threshold where their loyalty to YHWH's appointed leader hangs by a thread.
5: And YHWH said to Moses, 'Pass on before the people, taking with you some of the elders of Israel, and take in your hand the staff with which you struck the Nile, and go.' The elders of Israel serve as witnesses to the miracle — the same staff that struck the Nile (the instrument of the blood plague) now strikes the rock to produce water. The symbolic inversion is significant: the instrument of judgment on Egypt becomes the instrument of provision for Israel. The staff's dual function (plague-bringer + provision-giver) depends entirely on the direction in which YHWH commands it to be used.
6: 'Behold, I will stand before you there on the rock at Horeb, and you shall strike the rock, and water shall come out of it, and the people will drink.' And Moses did so, in the sight of the elders of Israel: YHWH standing on the rock at Horeb — the divine presence localized on the specific rock that will produce water. The identification of the location as Horeb (the alternative name for Sinai/the mountain of God) places this water miracle at the threshold of the covenant mountain. Paul identifies this rock with Christ in 1 Corinthians 10:4: 'they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ.' The ANE tradition of a divine figure dwelling within or upon a rock (gods of the mountain) is inverted here: YHWH stands on the rock not to be worshipped there but to provide through it.
7: And he called the name of the place Massah (testing) and Meribah (quarreling), because of the quarreling of the people of Israel, and because they tested YHWH by saying, 'Is YHWH among us or not?' The double place-naming encodes the theological verdict: Massah = test/temptation (from nassah), Meribah = contention/dispute (from rav/riv). The question 'Is YHWH among us or not?' (ha'yesh YHWH b'qirbeinu im-ayin) is the theological nadir of the water-crisis — the fundamental question of divine presence that the entire wilderness narrative is designed to answer. Psalms 95:8-9 cites Massah/Meribah as the paradigmatic failure of faith that Israel must not repeat; Hebrews 3:7-11 extends the warning to the church. The question of divine presence (ha'yesh YHWH b'qirbeinu?) is answered throughout the tabernacle narrative with the emphatic: yes, he dwells among you.
8: Then Amalek came and fought with Israel at Rephidim: The Amalekites are the first external military enemy Israel faces after the Exodus. In biblical genealogy, Amalek is the grandson of Esau through Eliphaz and his concubine Timna (Genesis 36:12) — making the Amalekites distant relatives of Israel through the Esau line. Amalekite territory is attested in Egyptian and Akkadian sources as the region of the Negev and northern Sinai; they appear as a nomadic semi-desert people who controlled key water and trade routes. The attack at Rephidim — immediately after the water-from-rock miracle — may be connected to competition over water resources in the Sinai. Deuteronomy 25:17-18 specifies that Amalek 'cut off your tail' (attacked the stragglers at the rear of the marching column), a classic nomadic raiding tactic.
9: So Moses said to Joshua, 'Choose for us men, and go out and fight with Amalek. Tomorrow I will stand on the top of the hill with the staff of God in my hand.' Joshua (Yehoshua = YHWH is salvation) makes his narrative debut here as military commander — his first appearance in the Exodus narrative since Exodus 24:13 (where he appears as Moses's attendant). The 'staff of God' (mateh ha-Elohim) is the staff of the plagues and the Red Sea crossing, now employed in warfare. Moses's position on the hilltop with the staff parallels the Egyptian pharaonic tradition of the king watching battle from an elevated position, but with a crucial difference: Moses is not directing the battle but interceding with the uplifted staff.
10: So Joshua did as Moses told him, and fought with Amalek, while Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill: Hur (mentioned here and in Exodus 24:14 as Aaron's co-deputy over the people) is identified in later rabbinic tradition as Miriam's son or husband. He is mentioned twice in Exodus and then disappears from the narrative. In the Chronicler's genealogy (1 Chronicles 2:19-20), Hur is identified as the son of Caleb and Ephrath, and the grandfather of Bezalel (the tabernacle craftsman) — which would make him Moses's family connection to the tabernacle's construction team.
11: Whenever Moses held up his hand, Israel prevailed, and whenever he lowered his hand, Amalek prevailed: The connection between Moses's uplifted hands and Israel's military fortunes is the theological principle of the passage: the battle belongs to YHWH and is mediated through Moses's intercession (uplifted hands = the classic prayer posture in the ancient Near East; cf. Psalms 28:2, Exodus 63:4, Exodus 141:2; 1 Timothy 2:8). The battle is not won by Israel's military superiority (Joshua was choosing men — implying a select force) but by sustained intercession from above. The hilltop position of Moses parallels Egyptian battle iconography where a deity or divine-king stands above the battle directing its outcome.
12: But Moses' hands grew weary, so they took a stone and put it under him, and he sat on it, while Aaron and Hur held up his hands, one on one side, and the other on the other side. So his hands were steady (emunah) until the going down of the sun: Emunah = steadiness/faithfulness/firmness (the word translated 'faith/faithfulness' throughout the OT; cf. Habakkuk 2:4 'the righteous shall live by emunah'). Moses's hands being emunah (steady/faithful) until sunset is the first occurrence of emunah as a narrative-action word rather than an abstract character quality. The community supporting the intercessor (Aaron and Hur holding Moses's arms) is the practical dimension of corporate prayer: sustained intercession requires the community's physical and spiritual support.
13: And Joshua overwhelmed (va'yachalosh) Amalek and his people with the sword's edge: Chalash = to weaken/overwhelm/defeat (a rare verb in the military sense); the decisive defeat of Amalek 'at the mouth of the sword' (lefi-charev = at the sword's edge/lip). The victory is decisive but not total — Amalek is not annihilated here, only defeated. The incomplete defeat at Rephidim is the beginning of the long conflict between Israel and Amalek that will require further action (Deuteronomy 25:19; 1 Sam 15).
14: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Write this as a memorial in a book and recite it in the ears of Joshua, that I will utterly blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven.' The command to write (k'tov zot zikaron b'sefer) is the first explicit writing command in the Exodus narrative after Moses's general writing of the Covenant Code (Exodus 24:4). The sefer (book/scroll) as the medium for permanent covenant memory is established here: memory requires inscription. The command to 'recite it in the ears of Joshua' links the written memory to oral transmission — the written and oral Torah work together in covenant pedagogy. The eschatological blotting out (machoh emcheh et-zecher Amalek) is a permanent covenantal decree.
15: And Moses built an altar and called the name of it, YHWH is my Banner (YHWH nissi): YHWH nissi = YHWH my banner/standard (nes = a pole or standard raised as a rallying point for troops in battle; cf. Isaiah 11:10,12; Numbers 21:8-9 — the bronze serpent on a 'nes'). The altar built at the battle-site names the theological principle learned: YHWH was Israel's true standard in the battle, raised high through Moses's intercession. The divine name as battle-standard anticipates the NT language of the cross as the nes/standard raised for all nations (John 12:32 — 'I, when I am lifted up, will draw all people to myself').
16: He said, 'A hand upon the throne of Yah (ki-yad al-kes Yah)! YHWH will have war with Amalek from generation to generation.' The most difficult verse in the passage — 'ki-yad al-kes Yah' is grammatically ambiguous. Possible readings: 'Because a hand is against the throne of Yah' (Amalek attacks YHWH's throne by attacking Israel); or 'A hand on the standard/sign — Yah swears' (an oath formula). The LXX reads 'with a hidden hand.' The theological point regardless of the exact translation: the Amalekite attack on Israel is treated as an attack on YHWH's royal authority, making the conflict cosmic rather than merely tribal. The divine oath of permanent warfare 'from generation to generation (l'dor dor)' establishes Amalek as the paradigmatic enemy of YHWH's covenant people.
